sonnet_review.reason: The adjective is evaluative and amplifies the significance of the rulings; it appears in the reporter’s voice. I did not flag it originally, but it’s a defensible bias observation.

sonnet_review.biasedText: A conservative appeals court in New Orleans last week gave the Trump administration a victory in its drive to lock up more immigrants.
sonnet_review.reason: The article foregrounds numerous defeats for the administration and sympathetic detainee anecdotes, while offering little detail on contrary rulings beyond brief mention. The slant claim is defensible, even if consistent with the story’s news peg. (The model also cited sympathetic details like a lawyer taking a case for free as contributing to this slant.)

sonnet_review.biasedText: drive to lock up more immigrants
sonnet_review.reason: “Drive to lock up” is loaded and more pejorative than neutral alternatives like “policy to detain.” It’s reasonable to see this as biased wording.
Using “jailing” instead of the more neutral “detaining” frames civil custody as punitive; “indefinitely” also heightens the tone. This is a defensible bias observation.

gpt_review.biasedText: The decisions amount to a sweeping legal rebuke of Trump’s immigration crackdown. Yet the administration has continued jailing people indefinitely even after courts ruled the policy was illegal.
gpt_review.reason: This framing uses charged language (“sweeping,” “jailing…indefinitely”) that emphasizes negative evaluation. It’s grounded in the text and is a reasonable bias call.

gpt_review.biasedText: Sheer scale of the lawsuits threatens to clog the judicial system
gpt_review.reason: Phrases like “sheer scale” and “threatens to clog” are emotive and metaphorical. While partially supported by data later, the wording itself is heightened; a defensible detection.
Highlighting this fact about selected cases humanizes detainees without broader context or statistics; it contributes to a sympathetic slant and is a reasonable observation.
